question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: Internal-only browser TLS trust warnings like your HR portal symptom were recorded in TI-1700005; root cause there was a renewed issuing/intermediate CA not being published to the internal AIA distribution point. Remediation recorded: publish the intermediate to AIA/CRL and force client refresh (gpupdate / certutil -pulse) which cleared client warnings

SOURCE 55:
Ticket summary:
Ticket name: KF80: Internal portal showing TLS certificate errors after PKI renewal
Ticket number: TI-1700005
Company: Customer Company 80 AG
Contact person: Contact Person 31
Owner: Employee 32
Source: Email
Type: Support Request
Created on: 11.02.25 08:30

Closure
Closure date: 19.02.25 11:48
Closure text: Intermediate CA certificate published correctly to AIA/CRL distribution points; affected services validated.

Activities
Emails:

11.02.25 08:30 KF80: Internal application throwing certificate errors Contact Person 31 Helpdesk
Hello,
Since this morning, our internal HR portal (https://hrportal.kf80.local) is showing certificate warnings in the browser when accessed from any client. The error in Chrome is "NET::ERR_CERT_AUTHORITY_INVALID" and in Edge it says the certificate is not trusted.
The portal was working fine yesterday. The only change I am aware of is that our PKI team renewed the internal Certificate Authority certificates over the weekend, which we were told would be transparent to users.
This is blocking access to HR records and the absence reporting system.
Thanks
Contact Person 31

11.02.25 08:35 Ticket created: TI-1700005
Confirmation.

11.02.25 08:40 Ticket priority High - operational impact on internal services.

11.02.25 08:55 Ticket assigned | TI-1700005
Assigned to Employee 32 (BU Infrastructure - PKI/Network).

11.02.25 13:15 Re: KF80: Internal application certificate errors Employee 32 Contact Person 31
Hello Contact Person 31,
Thanks. To investigate the post-renewal cert chain, could you please:
- Confirm what was renewed on the weekend (the issuing CA, the root, both?)
- Send the output of running on a client: certutil -urlfetch -verify against the portal cert (or just attach the cert chain shown in browser → Inspect → Security)
- Confirm whether this affects only this portal or also other internal HTTPS services

11.02.25 16:50 Re: KF80: Internal application certificate errors Contact Person 31 Employee 32
Hi,
The PKI team confirmed they renewed the issuing CA certificate (KF80-Issuing-CA-01) on Saturday evening. The root CA was not changed.
Browser inspection shows: Certificate is signed by KF80-Issuing-CA-01, but the issuing CA cert in the chain shows "Issuer's certificate not found" and the chain breaks at the issuing CA.
We checked two other internal HTTPS services and they are also showing similar warnings (one of them is our internal SharePoint farm).

19.02.25 11:48 Ticket closed | TI-1700005
Closure description:

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: VPN authentication failures where clients “could not verify the server identity” match TI-1700006: CheckPoint IKEv2 logs showed an EKU mismatch because the post‑renewal VPN server certificate was issued from a template that omitted the required IPsec/IKE EKU. Remediation recorded: fix the template, re‑issue the server cert, deploy to the concentrator and restart — affected users recovered

The user-side certificates were unchanged but the IKEv2 mutual authentication step now

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: Office Wi‑Fi missing on new/replacement devices was documented as an Intune/profile scoping issue in prior cases: Wi‑Fi profiles assigned to a compliance‑based dynamic group were “not applicable” for devices flagged non‑compliant (e.g., after a tightened BitLocker escrow policy); resolving actions included changing assignment scope so new devices received the profile

Hi,
- Devices: KF90-LT-3001, KF90-LT-3002, KF90-LT-3003
- Compliance: all three show "non-compliant" - flagged for "BitLocker recovery key not escrowed within 30 days" (we are looking at this in another ticket)
- Wi-Fi profile is assigned to "KF90-Compliant-Devices" dynamic group

13.03.25 16:48 Ticket closed | TI-1700010
Closure description:
Root cause: The Wi-Fi profile was deployed via assignment to a dynamic group "KF90-Compliant-Devices" whose membership rule was based on Intune compliance state. The MAM policy correctly enforced that work data could not be accessed on non-compliant devices, preventing email setup from completing.
The "loop"

SOURCE 93:
﻿
Ticket summary:
Ticket name: KF11: Intune NDES Connector lost communication
Ticket number: TI-722423
Company: Customer Company 11
Contact person: Contact Person 8
Owner: Employee 11
Origin: Phone
Type: Incident
Created on: 27.03.24 12:04
Closure
Closure date: 14.06.24 16:10
Closure text: Incident successfully resolved
Activities
Emails:
27.03.24 13:10 | TI-722423 | KF11: Intune NDES Connector lost communication | Employee 11 | Contact Person 8
Hello Contact Person 8, hello Contact Person 2,
Together with the network team, I adjusted the firewall rules and then reinstalled the connector – this time without proxy configuration. After the reinstallation, the connector was displayed as “green” again in Azure (this step is required so that it can properly communicate with the Intune service).
Since we had identified login issues, we temporarily excluded the service account from the MFA requirement.
After completing the installation, I removed the “Intune Admin” role from the service account again. If we need to reinstall the connector, this role must be temporarily reassigned.
During the installation, I followed the Microsoft guide
“Configure infrastructure to support SCEP certificate profiles with Microsoft Intune.”
Attached you will find logs to quickly verify whether the connector is operating correctly. See blueprint_1.
Please do not make any changes to the settings without prior coordination.
If issues occur during the Intune deployment, you may contact the Helpdesk or the WPS team at any time.

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: The ticket’s combination of (a) internal TLS chain trust failures, (b) VPN server‑identity/EKU failures, (c) Intune/profile delivery issues for new devices, and (d) mobile App‑Protection onboarding loops maps directly to multiple prior incidents that required PKI, VPN/network, Endpoint/Intune and Mobile/App‑Protection engineering actions — not routine helpdesk steps. The indexed cases above were handled by infrastructure/PKI and endpoint teams and involved certificate/template fixes, CA/AIA publication, re‑issuance/deployment of server certs, and Intune policy/assignment changes
